title: Learning shit as I want it.
body: Since I remember I always had a problem with structured learning. I think I never did my homework and always belived I am very bad at math. With age and experience, I noticed that the best way for me to learn is just by building stuff. I could watch 100 tutorials and nothing would stick, but give me a problem to slove and I will obsess over this until it‘s resolved. Now, I also firmly belive that formal education doesn‘t make sense anymore, the structure is old and it't not adapting to the current times fast enough, yet we still default to universities when we think about the credentials. I belive in the proof of work, literally, try to make stuff, fail, learn and repeat until you have something cool. The only viable concept in formal education is the idea of curriculum, the structured and logical learning plan a roadmap. What I also learned through the years is that I need a roadmap/ training plan to be consisted. We used to depend on universtities or bootcamps or online courses, but with enough understaning of your own learning style and AI, you can curate the curriculum yourself and optimize it for your life circumstances and your background. So yeah, this is my robotics roadmap, that is optimized for fun and building, using easily accesible online resources. Cheers!

title: Being a generalist is a cheat code.
body: This week I want to talk about being a generalist and why that‘s helpful when you‘re tinkering with anything. Since I remember, I was interested in absolutely everything and my interests changed every week. As a small girl, one week I wanted to be a dentist, then a movie director, then an electrician. I never had an issue with changing direction when something new caught me, and when I started my career I had a pretty easy time just following whatever interested me. In the beginning it‘s hard because you don‘t know what you don‘t know, you just need to grab something and get some depth in it. In my case that was data analysis, and once I had depth in one area it became very natural to build on top of that. That‘s how I moved from data analysis to data engineering, to software development, to product architecture, and then to being a forward deployed engineer solving customer problems. Through all of that I noticed that two things motivate me very deeply when learning anything: following what actually interests me, and having a real problem to solve. I think at the core every human being is wired like that, which is why gaming and cliffhanger-y Netflix series will always be popular — it‘s something that interests you, gives you a bit of a thrill, plus a puzzle to solve on top. That‘s also why I got interested in robotics in the first place — after a certain time, software only, purely virtual, was not enough for me. I got really interested in translating software into the physical world, and I think a lot of people have this natural progression, especially with some microelectronics background from university to lean on. The other layer I realized is how much it helps to approach a new area from what you already know. It sounds obvious, but it‘s surprising how certain areas translate — like the software development cycle translating to the hardware development cycle. There are nuances, but the background carries you. And on top of that, original solutions start appearing through being multi-dimensional. If I have knowledge in business, in serving the customer and resolving their pain, in software and data, and now in robotics, that‘s a full package for solving original, hard problems through a multifaceted background. Build log update: the driving part of the cleaning robot is being assembled. There‘s a problem with acceleration in the wheels — purely a hardware issue. It made me realize that hardware is much more difficult than software, mostly because of debugging. The bugs are a different flavor (usually it‘s electricity not flowing through a circuit, or something along those lines), but actually finding the exact part that isn‘t working is one of the hardest things for me in hardware so far.
